questions.q: What two quantities are plotted on a Hertzsprung-Russell (H-R) diagram, and how does the temperature axis run?
options: Mass versus age, with mass increasing to the right
Luminosity (vertical, brightest at top) versus surface temperature (horizontal), with temperature increasing to the LEFT
Distance versus brightness, with distance increasing upward
Radius versus luminosity, with radius increasing to the right
questions.explanation: The H-R diagram plots luminosity (brightest at top) against surface temperature or spectral class. The crucial trap: temperature increases to the LEFT, so hot blue O stars are on the left and cool red M stars on the right.

questions.q: Why are red giants very luminous despite being relatively cool?
options: They are extremely hot at their surface
They are very large, so their huge surface area radiates a large total power even though each square metre is cool
They are very close to Earth
They fuse iron in their cores, releasing extra energy
questions.explanation: Luminosity depends on both temperature and surface area (\(L \propto R^2 T^4\)). A red giant is cool but enormous, so its vast surface area makes the total power output huge. White dwarfs are the opposite: hot but tiny, hence dim.

questions.q: Two main-sequence stars have the same surface temperature, but star X is much more luminous than star Y. What can you conclude?
options: Star X is hotter than star Y
Star X is larger than star Y, because at the same temperature luminosity is set by surface area
Star Y is closer to Earth
Star X is moving toward us
questions.explanation: Same temperature means temperature cannot explain the brightness difference. Since luminosity depends on both temperature and surface area, the more luminous star must have the larger radius. Naming this principle is the mark-earning move when comparing stellar sizes.

questions.q: What balance keeps a main-sequence star stable (hydrostatic equilibrium)?
options: Magnetic pressure outward balancing electric attraction inward
The inward pull of gravity balanced by the outward radiation pressure generated by fusion
The outward pull of gravity balanced by inward fusion pressure
Neutron degeneracy pressure balancing the strong nuclear force
questions.explanation: A stable main-sequence star is a balance: gravity pulls the star inward, while the radiation (and thermal) pressure from core fusion pushes outward, and the two are equal. When fuel runs low this balance breaks and the core contracts.

questions.q: A massive blue star high on the main sequence and a small red star low on it are compared. Which statement is correct?
options: The massive blue star has more fuel, so it lives the longest
The massive blue star fuses fuel fast and hot, so it is more luminous but has a SHORTER main-sequence lifetime
The small red star is hotter and shorter-lived
Both stars have identical lifetimes regardless of mass
questions.explanation: Greater mass means stronger gravity, requiring faster, hotter fusion to hold the star up — making it bright and blue but burning through fuel disproportionately fast. So despite having more fuel, massive stars 'live fast, die young'.

questions.q: In the proton-proton chain and the CNO cycle, what is the role of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the CNO cycle?
options: They are the fuel that is consumed to make energy
They are the final products of the cycle
They act as catalysts — consumed at one step and regenerated at another, so they are not used up
They are waste products that must be removed
questions.explanation: In the CNO cycle, carbon captures protons and transforms through nitrogen and oxygen isotopes, finally releasing a helium-4 and regenerating the original carbon-12. C, N and O are catalysts — enabling the reaction without net consumption. The fuel is four protons; the product is one helium-4.

questions.q: How do the NET products of the proton-proton chain and the CNO cycle compare?
options: The pp chain makes helium while the CNO cycle makes carbon
They are identical: four protons become one helium-4 nucleus plus two positrons, two neutrinos and gamma rays
The CNO cycle produces no neutrinos
The pp chain produces iron, the CNO cycle produces oxygen
questions.explanation: Both routes have the same net result: \(4\,{}^{1}\mathrm{H} \to {}^{4}\mathrm{He} + 2e^{+} + 2\nu + \gamma\). The differences are the pathway, the core temperature required, and that the CNO cycle needs pre-existing carbon — not the products. The CNO cycle does NOT make carbon (A).

questions.q: Step 1 of the pp chain releases 1.44 MeV. Using \(E = mc^2\), with \(1\ \mathrm{eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{J}\) and \(c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\), the mass converted is closest to:
options: \(1.4 \times 10^{6}\ \mathrm{kg}\)
\(9.0 \times 10^{16}\ \mathrm{kg}\)
questions.explanation: Convert first: \(1.44 \times 10^{6}\ \mathrm{eV} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 2.3 \times 10^{-13}\ \mathrm{J}\). Then \(m = E/c^2 = 2.3 \times 10^{-13} / 9.0 \times 10^{16} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-30}\ \mathrm{kg}\). The \(2.3 \times 10^{-13}\) value (B) is the energy in joules, not the mass.

questions.q: Using the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve, why is iron the heaviest element built by fusion in a star?
options: Iron nuclei are too large for the strong force to act on
Iron-56 sits at the peak of the curve; fusing it into anything heavier would move down the curve to lower binding energy per nucleon, absorbing energy rather than releasing it, so fusion halts
Iron does not exist inside stars
Iron is radioactive and decays before it can fuse
questions.explanation: Fusion releases energy only while climbing the curve toward the iron-56 peak (the most tightly bound nucleus). Fusing past iron means descending to lower binding energy per nucleon, which requires energy input. A star cannot power itself this way, so fusion stops at iron — then the iron core collapses, triggering a supernova.

questions.q: Where were the elements heavier than iron (such as gold and uranium) synthesised?
options: In the Big Bang, in the first few minutes of the universe
By ordinary fusion in the cores of main-sequence stars
In supernova explosions, by rapid neutron capture
They have always existed and were never created
questions.explanation: The Big Bang made only hydrogen, helium and a trace of lithium; stellar fusion builds elements up to iron. Elements heavier than iron form in supernovae via rapid neutron capture and are scattered into space. 'Iron is the heaviest element' is true only with the qualifier 'made by fusion'.
